The program is an outgrowth of the effort begun over 20 years ago in the Aircraft Nuclear Propulsion program to make a molten-salt reactor power plant for aircraft.
A molten-salt reactor - the Aircraft Reactor Experiment - was operated at ORNL in 1954 as part of the ANP program.
Fuel for this type of reactor would be $^{233}\mathrm{UF}_4$ dissolved in a salt that is a mixture of LiF and $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ , but $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ or plutonium could be used for startup.
A major program activity through 1969 was the operation of the Molten-Salt Reactor Experiment.
The MSRE operated at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and produced $7.3\mathrm{MW}$ of heat.
The initial fuel contained 0.9 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{UF_4}$ , $5\%$ $\mathrm{ZrF_4}$ , $29\%$ $\mathrm{BeF_2}$ , and $65\%$ ${}^{7}\mathrm{LiF}$ ; the uranium was about $33\%$ ${}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ .

We concluded that this objective had been achieved and that the MSRE had shown that molten-fluoride reactors can be operated at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ without corrosive attack on either the metal or graphite parts of the system, the fuel is stable, reactor equipment can operate satisfactorily at these conditions, xenon can be removed rapidly from molten salts, and, when necessary, the radioactive equipment can be repaired or replaced.

The second phase of MSRE operation began in August 1968, when a small facility in the MSRE building was used to remove the original uranium charge from the fuel salt by treatment with gaseous $\mathbf{F}_2$ .
In six days of fluorination, $221~\mathrm{kg}$ of uranium was removed from the molten salt and loaded onto absorbers filled with sodium fluoride pellets.
After the fuel was processed, a charge of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ was added to the original carrier salt, and in October 1968 the MSRE became the world's first reactor to operate on $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
The nuclear characteristics of the MSRE with the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ were close to the predictions, and the reactor was quite stable.
In September 1969, small amounts of $\mathrm{PuF_3}$ were added to the fuel to obtain some experience with plutonium in a molten-salt reactor.
The MSRE was shut down permanently December 12, 1969, so that the funds supporting its operation could be used elsewhere in the research and development program.

In late 1967, however, a one-fluid breeder became feasible because of the development of processes that use liquid bismuth to isolate protactinium and remove rare earths from a salt that also contains thorium.

In the congressional authorization report on the AEC's programs for FY-1973, the Joint Committee on Atomic Energy recommended that the molten-salt reactor be reappraised so that a decision could be made about its continuation and the level of funding appropriate for it.

A considerable effort during the reporting period covered by this progress report was devoted to the preparation of ORNL-4812, "The Development Status of Molten-Salt Breeder Reactors," a 416-page report that contains the results of our review.

The first core was very similar to the core of the MSDR except that a $3/8$ -in.-wall, 6-in.-OD graphite tube was put into the center of each graphite core cell.
It is $13^{1}/8$ in. $^2$ , made up of four corner posts, some slabs of graphite with flow passages between them, and the graphite downcomer tube in the center.
A 4-in.-diam nozzle introduces lead from the plenum into the center of each downcomer.
The lead velocity leaving this nozzle is 32 fps, and the salt enters the annulus around the nozzle with a velocity of 8.3 fps.
The pressure in the lead plenum to produce this flow is 80 psi.
Mixing of the lead and salt results in a pressure rise of 63 psi which, with the static pressure of the mixed liquids, produces a pressure of 178 psi at the bottom of the core.
In this design, the lead enters the top of the reactor at a temperature of $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and leaves at a temperature of $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The temperature of the salt is $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ at the top of the core and, of course, is the same $(1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F})$ as the lead at the bottom.

The cell is still $13^{1/8}$ in.², but it is constructed of four extrusions which, when assembled, form a square with an $11^{1/2}$ -in.-diam hole in the center.
Inside this hole are two concentric graphite cylinders, each having a wall thickness of $17/16$ in.
In the center of the inner cylinder is a 3-in.-diam rod.
This cell provides two annuli of $3/8$ -in. thickness and an outer annulus of $7/8$ -in. thickness.
The two inner annuli provide riser channels for the salt, while the outer annulus provides the downcomer and contains 1.5 volumes of lead per volume of salt.
Figure 1.2 shows both a plan and elevation of this cell arrangement.
At 8-ft intervals in the outer annulus, there are turning vanes which impart a rotational component to the mixture of lead and salt.
This rotation generates a 1-g centrifugal field which separates the lead from the salt by the time the mixed stream reaches the bottom
The salt collects in a plenum (approximately 2 in. deep) and flows back up through the two annular risers in each cell.

In order to reduce the neutron absorptions in the lead in this core, the ratio of lead to salt is made 1.5:1, necessitating a $200^{\circ}\mathrm{F} \Delta t$ in the lead and thus raising the lead temperature to $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and the salt temperature to $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

The corresponding lead plenum pressure to attain this velocity is 267 psi, a value which we consider to be excessive because of the stresses imposed on the reactor vessel.
These peripheral cells are squares $13^{1/4}$ in. on a side with an 11-in.-diam hole in the center.

Lead with a velocity of 25 fps is introduced into an annulus through an 8-in.-OD nozzle centered around the central graphite cylinder.

In the course of these studies we established the need for a computer program that could calculate the time-dependent behavior of a fluid-fueled reactor, including the effects of significant changes in the neutron spectrum caused by changes in the fuel composition, and we developed the HISTRY-2 option of the ROD code to meet this need.
In the current six-month period, the HISTRY-2 option has been made operational, and the plutonium studies have reached a culmination.

Our results indicate that plutonium is a desirable fuel for MSCR's: if assigned a value of 9.9 $\mathbb{S} / \mathbb{kg}$ fissile, plutonium gives somewhat lower fuel cycle costs in an MSR than does enriched uranium.

The losses due to fission products and protacitinium in a batch-processed MSCR reduce the breeding ratio below 1.0, but even so most of the fissions over the lifetime of the reactor are in bred $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .

(It is presently uneconomical to recover plutonium from MSR salt.) For our MSCR studies we used a simplified reactor model consisting of a spherical graphite core with a 0.12 salt volume fraction surrounded by a 78.4-cm reflector with a 0.01 salt fraction.

(Earlier studies had shown that the spherical model adequately represents the actual cylindrical core.) The core diameter was adjusted in each case to give a peak damage flux equivalent to a graphite life of 30 years in a 2250-MW(t) plant operating at a 0.8 load factor.
The initial fissile loading and subsequent feed for an MSCR could be enriched uranium, plutonium, or $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ from other reactors.
The presence of much $^{240}\mathrm{Pu}$ tends to harden the neutron spectrum and increase the critical loading.

However, the pump was placed in routine operation again on August 10, 1972, and has now accumulated about $5800\mathrm{hr}$ of operation at MSR-FCL-2 design conditions of $4800\mathrm{rpm}$ , pumping 4 gpm of sodium fluoroborate at $850^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
A wattmeter was installed to measure the power input to the variable-speed pump drive, a 5-hp Adjustospede system.
The input power was measured at $3.7\mathrm{kW}$ (4.96 hp) with the pump operating at MSR-FCL-2 design conditions.
The data were used to determine pump efficiency, which at the present relatively low flow rate is $8\%$ .
At a pump design flow rate of $30\mathrm{gpm}$ an efficiency of $40\%$ is anticipated.

The lower shaft seal for the ALPHA pump was equipped with Viton O-rings $^{13,14}$ in August 1971, after the original Buna N O-rings failed by attack from the effluent, a dilute mixture of $\mathrm{BF}_3$ ( $<0.1\%$ ) in helium.
To obtain performance information concurrent with pump operation, sample rings of Viton (Parker compound 77-545) and Buna N were installed in the effluent from the lower seal region of the pump.
The O-rings have been examined after 2000 and $4200\mathrm{hr}$ exposure, and no apparent degradation of the Viton has occurred.
The Buna N was hardened and cracked at $2000\mathrm{hr}$ exposure.

An oil leak from the upper end of the ALPHA pump shaft caused a shutdown of corrosion loop MSR-FCL-2 on April 28, 1972, after nearly 5300 hr of satisfactory pump and loop operation.
About 1 gal of oil was lost from the lubrication system before the leak was stopped.
The oil leak occurred at the upper end of the pump in a soft-soldered joint between the bore of the Hastelloy N pump shaft and a 304 stainless steel plug on the oil guide tube.
The oil system was being operated at normal design conditions with a flow rate of 0.2 psig.

This square-root dependence has a sound theoretical basis, but the literature contains many reports that at pressures below about 30 to 100 torr the dependence changes from the square-root relation to the first-power (direct proportionality) relation.
The square-root dependence is quite good to pressures well under $10^{-3}$ torr.

Nickel was used because the oxidation potential expressed as $P_{\mathrm{H_2O}} / P_{\mathrm{H_2}}$ is about 100 at temperatures of interest, and oxidation by impurity water or air leaks would not be expected to form any impedance to flow.
Stainless steel and indeed chromium alloys generally have an oxidation potential near $10^{-5}$ to form $\mathrm{Cr_2O_3}$ (chromium sesquioxide) from chromium metal.
An 18-8 stainless steel was studied as a metal which was expected to form a protective oxide coating.
After a light coating of oxide was permitted to form, the permeation rate decreased by a factor of 8.

To study the effect of one "best chance" oxide, a nickel tube was electroplated with a 1-mil coating of chromium, and the surface of this film was oxidized within the permeation apparatus to $\mathrm{Cr_2O_3}$ .
Oxidation at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ showed only a relatively small decrease in permeability from that expected for nickel.
Since Flint $^8$ had shown that more effective films could develop on steel under oxidation at higher temperatures, the chromium plate was oxidized at about $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The subsequent measurements of permeability were encouraging; a marked reduction in permeation (to about $1\%$ of the preoxidation value at 1 torr and $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) was observed, and the pressure dependence was found to be near first power.
Much, but not all, of the decrease remained after a thermal cycle to room temperature and back to $625^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Indeed, as this report goes to press, we have preliminary evidence that the film that formed readily in the permeability apparatus by oxidation of Incoloy 800 shows a markedly reduced permeability and a pressure dependence near first power.

In practice the most sensitive region of the titration curve is found at iodine concentrations corresponding to about $200\mathrm{mV}$ potential between the electrodes, and the end-point potential is selected at about this value.
An electrode was constructed of 0.040-in. platinum wire approximately $3/8$ in. long attached at right angles to a $1/8$ -in. rotating shaft.

By a systematic variation of the parameters associated with this electrode, we have found that good results can be obtained with $7 \mathrm{~mm}$ of 0.040-in. platinum exposed and rotated at $1800 \mathrm{rpm}$ with a polarizing current of $35 \mu \mathrm{A}$ .

Under these conditions the response is about $15 \mathrm{mV}$ per microgram of water in approximately $100 \mathrm{ml}$ of titrating solution, and the output voltage appears essentially free of noise on the damped meter of the instrument.

In the titration of test portions of methanol containing about $500 \mu \mathrm{g}$ of water, values of $500 \pm 3 \mu \mathrm{g}$ were obtained, whereas reproducibility to better than $1 \mu \mathrm{g}$ was obtained with smaller injections ( $\sim 30 \mu \mathrm{g}$ ).
Katz and Pitt<sup>10</sup> have described this technique as a very sensitive means of determining organics in aqueous solution, and we have considered using this in an inorganic melt.

Based on analogous chemical behavior, one might guess that $\mathrm{CeO_2}$ would be very insoluble in fluoride melts, while $\mathrm{Ce(III)}$ would be soluble even in the presence of $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ .
The absorption spectra, in either melt at $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , in the spectral region of 600 to $200\mathrm{nm}$ , consist of three peaks located at 292, 244, and $224\mathrm{nm}$ .

The spectrum corresponds to the absorption spectrum seen for $\mathrm{Ce(III)}$ in $\mathrm{CaF_2}$ crystals. $^{11}$ No fluorescence of the melt has been observed, but the solidified salt did fluoresce.
The molar absorptivity of the most intense peak at $292\mathrm{nm}$ is estimated to be 700 liters $\mathrm{mole}^{-1}\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ .
Based on the absorbance of the $292\mathrm{nm}$ peak, $3\mu \mathrm{eq}$ of some material in the salt was oxidized.
Under our experimental conditions, down to $0.1\mu \mathrm{mol}$ of $\mathrm{Ce(III)}$ per gram of sample could be determined by absorption spectral measurement.

The evidence to date would indicate that $\mathrm{CeO_2}$ is not as strong an oxidant in these melts as, for example, MnF $_3$ , but $\mathrm{CeO_2}$ should be strong enough to oxidize $\mathrm{Ni}^0$ , $\mathrm{Fe}^0$ , etc., that might be present in such melts and that in the past have created problems in analysis when present with $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ .
Initially only $\mathrm{Te}^{2-}$ or $\mathrm{Te}^0$ has been considered.

The spectra of the resultant rose-colored solution probably arise from the $\mathrm{Te}^{2-}$ ion and consist of a moderately broad peak at $520\mathrm{nm}$ and a very intense absorption in the ultraviolet below 350 nm.
The spectra are similar but not identical to that reported by Gruen et al. $^{12}$ for $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{Te}$ and $\mathrm{Cs}_2\mathrm{Te}$ in melts that were essentially chloride media.
We do not know the sensitivity of the absorbance peak at $520\mathrm{nm}$ , but we estimate the molar absorptivity to be between 25 and 50 liters $\mathrm{mole}^{-1}\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ .
The solubility of either telluride, therefore, is believed to exceed $1000\mathrm{ppm}$ in LiF-NaF-KF at $525^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The dissolved $\mathrm{Te}^{2-}$ ion is reasonably stable in an inert atmosphere at least for 24 hr, but has been found to be easily oxidized by exposure of the melt to air, possibly an oxidation via $\mathrm{O}_2$ or by the addition of $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ .

The reddish-yellow solution has an essentially featureless ultraviolet absorption peak which appears similar to that obtained for $\mathrm{Li}_3\mathrm{Bi}$ in molten LiCl-LiF (80-20 mole%).[13] No absorption spectrum, or color, was observed when Li-Pb alloy was in contact with molten LiCl, and it seems probable that no unusual "plumbide" species were present in the salt melt.

text: Our materials work is currently involved with several important areas including (1) gaining a better understanding of the intergranular cracking of Hastelloy N that occurred in the MSRE fuel system and a method of controlling this cracking in future reactors, (2) developing a graphite with improved dimensional stability under radiation, (3) development of surface sealing methods for reducing the permeability of graphite to $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ , (4) modification of the composition of Hastelloy N to obtain an alloy that is more resistant to neutron irradiation, (5) evaluation of Hastelloy N for use in steam generators, (6) construction of a molybdenum system for use with bismuth and fluoride salts, and (7) the evaluation of other materials for use as structural materials in the chemical processing plant.
Thus the problem of dimensional stability cannot be separated from the requirement of low permeability to $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ .
Pyrolytic carbon coatings derived from propene are currently being studied as a means of reducing the permeability to $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ .

The program to develop a modified Hastelloy N with improved resistance to radiation indicated that alloys with additions of 1.5 to $2.0\%$ titanium were most promising, and recent efforts have concentrated on these.

The oxidized specimen was heated to $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for about $7\mathrm{hr}$ .
With the exception of the foil held at $750^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , thick black reaction layers formed on both the Hastelloy N foil and cell surfaces.
The foil exposed at $750^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ did not have a visible reaction product on the surface.
Reaction products were removed from the cell after the $750^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ exposure.
An extensive study of the nickel-tellurium system was made by Barstad et al.,[12] who identified beta, gamma, and delta nickel tellurides.
The beta field extends from $\mathrm{Ni}_3\mathrm{Te}_2$ ( $\mathrm{NiTe}_{0.67}$ ) to $\mathrm{NiTe}_{0.70}$ .
At $\mathrm{Ni}_3\mathrm{Te}_2$ , a monoclinic structure is observed with the angle $\beta = 91.22^{\circ}$ .
With increasing Te concentration, $\beta$ becomes $90^{\circ}$ to give an orthorhombic structure at $\mathrm{NiTe}_{0.69}$ .
At the composition $\mathrm{NiTe}_{0.70}$ (very slowly cooled) the continuing changes in the $a$ and $b$ parameters result in a tetragonal structure.
The gamma phase is located at $\mathrm{NiTe}_{0.77}$ and is orthorhombic.
Two chromium tellurides are reported in the ASTM Powder Diffraction File. $\alpha$ -CrTe is hexagonal with an approximate homogeneity range of 50 to $54\%$ Te.
We identified $\mathrm{NiTe}_2$ as the major phase formed by the reaction of Hastelloy N with tellurium at $412^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in the Knudsen cell.

The foil reacted at $412^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and subsequently held at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for 96 hr contained $\mathrm{NiTe}_{0.69}$ and an additional strong phase which is probably a high-parameter monoclinic $\beta$ -CrTe intermetallic.
No $\mathrm{NiTe}_2$ was detected at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
As previously mentioned, the foil exposed at $750^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for 725 hr did not have a removable reaction product.

However, the reaction product removed from the Hastelloy N effusion cell after the $750^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ exposure did provide a good x-ray-diffraction pattern. $\beta$ -CrTe and Hastelloy N were present in this reaction product, but nickel tellurides were not detectable.
The Hastelloy N-type lines probably resulted from decomposition of the nickel and (possibly) mixed tellurides originally formed at $412^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ rather than from the original alloy.
Metallographic examination of these foils expectedly revealed reaction layers along the surfaces of the foils reacted at 412 and $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Electron microprobe analysis showed the reaction layer on the $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ foil to consist of Ni, Mo, and Te with less than $1\%$ Cr.
The foil exposed at $750^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ after the $412^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ reaction was markedly different from those exposed at the lower temperatures.
Figure 10.23 shows the microstructure of this extensively reacted foil with striated particles along the edges.
